coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots inducing formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP to improve the piezoelectric performance
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Ping
Xu, Shunjian
Zhong, Wei
Fu, Haiyan
Luo, Yongping
Xiao, Zonghu
Zhang, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Flexible piezoelectric sensors (FPS) have been widely applied to intelligent robot, human/machine interaction and human health measurement system, etc. To improve the piezoelectric performance of polyvinylidene fluoride-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced to form PVDF-HFP/CQDs piezoelectric film. Owing to their large specific surface area (96.3 m²/g) and abundant surface defects, CQDs provide mass potential induced active points for the formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP, and rich surface functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2, etc) which could promote chemical bond with PVDF-HFP. The content of β phase in the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film is increased by 14.4 % compared with that in the PVDF-HFP film. Thereby, PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS exhibit better sensitivity (36.6 mV/g) than PVDF-HFP based FPS (25.7 mV/g) with good linearity (R2 > 99 %). Besides, the FPS show excellent mechanical stability under repeated dynamic loading. On this basis, some potential applications of the PVDF-HFP/CQDs based self-powered FPS were explored, including anti-touch alarm system, finger bending detection, voice recognition and vibration monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
PVDF-HFP
β Phase formation
Induced polarization
Flexible piezoelectric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of PVDF-HFP/CQDs film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Project of Xinyu University
Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by Education Department of Jiangxi Province ( GJJ191041 ; KJLD13100 ; GJJ191052 ; GJJ191046 ; GJJ171077 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province ( 20202BABL204023 ) and Scientific Research Project of Xinyu University ( XJZD1902 ; XJ1902 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS.
Schematic diagram of the electromechanical measurement system.
(a,b) SEM images and (c,d) AFM images of PVDF-HFP and PVDF-HFP/CQDs, TEM images of (e) PVDF-HFP/CQDs and (f) CQDs (inset: particle size distribution).
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR specpra of PVDF-HFP and PVDF-HFP/CQDs films, (c) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, (d) DTA cooling curves of films.
(a) Isotherm linear plot and (b) BET surface area plot of CQDs.

Real-time output voltages of (a) PVDF-HFP/CQDs and (b) PVDF-HFP based FPS under repeating different forces, (c) applied force-voltage curves of FPS, (d) mechanical durability of PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS.
Photographs (inset: schematic diagram) and real-time output voltages of (a,b) anti-touch alarm system, (c,d) finger bending detection, (e,f) voice recognition and (g,h) vibration monitoring.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots inducing formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP to improve the piezoelectric performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinyu Institute of New Energy, Xinyu University, Xinyu, 338004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinyu Institute of New Energy, Xinyu University, Xinyu 338004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs doped PVDF-HFP film with porous structure was firstly fabricated for self-powered flexible piezoelectric sensors.

Flexible piezoelectric sensors (FPS) have been widely applied to intelligent robot, human/machine interaction and human health measurement system, etc. To improve the piezoelectric performance of polyvinylidene fluoride-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced to form PVDF-HFP/CQDs piezoelectric film. Owing to their large specific surface area (96.3 m²/g) and abundant surface defects, CQDs provide mass potential induced active points for the formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP, and rich surface functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2, etc) which could promote chemical bond with PVDF-HFP. The content of β phase in the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film is increased by 14.4 % compared with that in the PVDF-HFP film. Thereby, PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS exhibit better sensitivity (36.6 mV/g) than PVDF-HFP based FPS (25.7 mV/g) with good linearity (R2 > 99 %). Besides, the FPS show excellent mechanical stability under repeated dynamic loading. On this basis, some potential applications of the PVDF-HFP/CQDs based self-powered FPS were explored, including anti-touch alarm system, finger bending detection, voice recognition and vibration monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible piezoelectric sensors (FPS), with high sensitivity, dynamic response, low energy consumption and even self-powered characteristic, have been widely applied to intelligent robot, human/machine interaction and human health measurement system, etc. [1]. Among the polymer piezoelectric materials, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and its derivatives, such as polyvinylidene fluoride-trifluoroethylene (PVDF-TrFE) and polyvinylidene fluoride-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP), have become outstanding representatives, due to their advantages of high flexibility, excellent electromechanical coupling, good biocompatibility, large optical transparency and wide response range [2,3]. Hou et al. [4] implemented the PVDF sensor to collect the data of pulse waves and respiration signals. Li et al. [5] investigated the sensing property of PVDF/ionic liquid composite under different ambient humidities, and found that voltage responses of the sensor were rarely influenced by the humidity, but strongly related to the content of ionic liquid. Lo et al. [6] prepared 3D stacked near‐field electrospun nanoporous PVDF-TrFE nanofibers as self-powered smart sensing in gait big data analytics, and the electrical voltage output performance was enhanced more than 2.7 times compared with primitive PVDF-TrFE nanofiber. Yuan et al. [7] reported a 3D-printed PVDF-TrFE piezoelectric film coated with one pair of dislocated interdigital electrode to produce multiple, alternatively tilt-polarized regions in the cross-sectional area, which exhibits quite high sensitivity to an external stress stimulation. PVDF is a kind of semi-crystalline polymer with at least five crystal phases of α, β, γ, δ and ε, but its electrical activity is mainly determined by the β phase [8]. Therefore, to obtain high content of β phase with specific conformation becomes a contributing way to enhance the piezoelectric property of PVDF. The typical strategy is to employ the polarization process to make the dipoles orderly arranged after the material is formed, with α phase of PVDF changing into β phase. The common technologies include stretching polarization, electric polarization and thermal polarization, etc. [9,10]. Recently, nanoparticle induced polarization is developed to facilitate the formation of β phase, due to its advantage of synchronization with material formation. BaTiO3 [11,12], ZnO [13], TiO2 [14], carbon black [8], graphene [15,16] and carbon nanotubes [17,18], have been demonstrated to have induced polarization effect by previous reports.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have good photoluminescence, light bleaching, chemical stability and wide range of raw materials, showing unique charm in the fields of fluorescence labeling, biological imaging and sensing, and light-emitting diodes [19–21]. CQDs can be regarded as a kind of shell-core material. The central carbon core is amorphous or sp2-sp3 hybrid crystal, and the spherical shell is rich in defects and surface functional groups, including carboxyl (COOH), amino (NH2), hydroxyl (OH), carbonyl (CO), cyano group (CN), sulfonic group (HSO3) and epoxy group (CH(O)CH), etc. [22]. These functional groups not only enable CQDs to have good dispersion in solvent, but also provide active sites for their bonding with other materials. Some studies have shown that grafting epoxy and carboxyl groups on the surface of multiwalled carbon nanotube can effectively strengthen the reaction with polymer chains of PVDF, thus improving the interfacial bonding and dispersion [17]. He et al. [23] constructed CQDs/PVDF-TrFE hybrid with multicolor photoluminescence for harvesting the environmental available kinetic mechanical energy. The open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current outputs reached up to 29.6 V cm−2 and 550 nA cm−2, respectively. Ma et al. [24] reported a similar poling-free piezoelectric system based on PVDF/CQDs composite for nanogenerators. There also exist some references on PVDF/CQDs hybrids for other applications. Jafari et al. [25] prepared CQDs/PVDF electrospun membranes for air gap membrane distillation. In this study, CQDs are firstly introduced into PVDF-HFP as a dispersion phase for flexible piezoelectric sensors. The larger specific surface area and abundant surface defects of CQDs will provide mass potential induced active points for the formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP. Moreover, the rich surface functional groups of CQDs in the shell will promote the formation of chemical bond between the filler and the substrate, which effectively strengthens the interface bond. Hence, it can be predicted that the incorporation of CQDs will improve the piezoelectric performance and mechanical stability of PVDF-HFP, and then promote the application of PVDF-HPF based FPS.

The CQDs were prepared through hydrothermal synthesis according to the previous report [26]. Briefly, the Teflon reactor filling with cotton fiber (300 mg), urea (150 mg) and ultrapure water (35 mL) reacted in the oven for 15 h at the temperature of 200 °C. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 min and filtration with 0.22 μm membranes, the initial CQDs solution was obtained. Then, the CQDs solution was purified with dialysis of hydrolysis membrane (1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the purified CQDs solution was freeze-dried to get solid CQDs for further usage.

The preparation process of PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS is shown in Fig. 1 . PVDF-HFP (1.5 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of acetone/dimethyl formamide (DMF) solvent with the volume ratio of 3:2 to obtain the pure PVDF-HFP solution. Then 2 mg CQDs was mixed into PVDF-HFP solution to get the PVDF-HFP/CQDs solution. The PVDF-HFP/CQDs solution was spin-coated on the PEN-ITO electrode at 1000 rpm for 60 s. After the solvent volatilized, the obtained PVDF-HFP/CQDs film was annealed at 120 ℃ for 1 h. Simultaneously, the pure PVDF-HFP film was prepared as the reference by the same method.

Sandwich-like FPS were assembled by a simple lamination method. In brief, another PEN-ITO (10 mm * 15 mm) was physically attached as top electrode on the piezoelectric film (PVDF-HFP or PVDF-HFP/CQDs, 15 mm * 15 mm). The edges of the FPS were sealed with 3M adhesive tape to make the structure compact. Then one corner of the film was wiped off with acetone to expose the bottom PEN-ITO for the lead. The effective area of the piezoelectric film was 10 mm * 10 mm.

The morphologies of the prepared films were observed by Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Nova Nano SEM450, FEI) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, SPM-9600, Shimadzu). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL) was used to record the morphologies of the CQDs and films. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 advance, Bruker) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet IS50, Thermo Scientific) spectroscopy were carried out to evaluate the structural properties of the films. Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA) measurement was performed on the synchronized thermal analyzer (STA2500, NETZSCH) in the atmosphere of N2 gas. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm was measured to detect the specific surface area of CQDs by specific surface area analyzer (TriStar II 3020, Micromeritics). The output voltage signals of FPS under different simulated forces were measured by a self-constructed electromechanical platform. As shown from the schematic diagram of the test system in Fig. 2 , the FPS was attached to a commercial mechanical sensor (M6x1, Qisheng, China), and a stepper motor (FSL40, Fuyu, China) was used to apply compressive force to the FPS. The force exerted on the FPS was obtained by the commercial mechanical sensor. The charge signal generated by the FPS was converted into the voltage signal by a charge-voltage converter (VK102, Weijingyi, China), which could be read by an oscilloscope (UTD7102BG, UNI-T, China). The sensitivity of the charge-voltage converter was 100 pC/V. The frequency of stimulation force used was set to 1 Hz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of PVDF-HFP/CQDs film
ce:section.ce:para: FE-SEM morphology of the prepared pure PVDF-HFP film is shown in Fig. 3 (a). It is evidently seen that abundant holes ranging from 1 μm to 3 μm are evenly distributed in the film, which originate from the slow evaporation process of the solvent. The same morphology of PVDF-HFP film has been reported in Zhong Lin Wang's literature [27]. As shown from the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film in Fig. 3(b), the morphology of this film does not change with the addition of CQDs, and the size of holes is close to that of the PVDF-HFP film. Larger views of the holes in the films are shown in insets of Fig. 3(a) and (b), which show that some PVDF-HFP wires that have not been completely broken by the solvent airflow are still hanging in the middle of the hole. Fig. 3(c) and (d) present AFM images of pure PVDF-HFP film and PVDF-HFP/CQDs film, respectively. The morphologies and hole sizes of the porous films are consistent with the FE-SEM results. To demonstrated the existence of CQDs in PVDF-HFP, TEM morphology of PVDF-HFP/CQDs is observed in Fig. 3(e), revealing that tori-spherical dots with about 3 nm in size are uniformly dispersed in PVDF-HFP. In order to confirm that the tori-spherical dots in PVDF-HFP are CQDs, the TEM image of CQDs is shown in Fig. 3(f). The synthesized CQDs are also tori-spherical dots with uniform size that are well dispersed in aqueous solution. By randomly measuring 100 particles, the size distribution of CQDs is shown in inset of Fig. 3(f). The average size is calculated to be 2.78 ± 0.32 nm, which is consistent with the result of tori-spherical dots in PVDF-HFP.

The structural properties of the PVDF-HFP and PVDF-HFP/CQDs films are characterized by XRD in Fig. 4 (a). Two peaks at 18.9° and 20.9° appear in the XRD spectrum, which are ascribed to the α phase of (020) crystal plane and β phase of (110) (200) crystal planes, respectively [28]. Owing to the existence of CQDs, the intensity ratio of β(20.9°)/α(18.9°) in PVDF-HFP/CQDs film is enhanced compared with that in PVDF-HFP film, which is beneficial to improve the piezoelectric properties of the film. To further confirm the crystal structures of the films, FT-IR spectra are also acquired in Fig. 4(b). α phase of PVDF-HFP is easily detected due to its characteristic absorption bands at 615, 763, 795, and 975 cm−1, while the peaks at 840 and 1276 cm−1 indicate the existence of β phase in PVDF-HFP [15]. Furthermore, the absorption intensities of 840 and 763 cm−1 are commonly utilized to quantify the relative content of electroactive phase in PVDF-HFP [29]. Assuming that FT-IR absorption conforms to the Lambert-Beer law, the relative percentage of β phase in PVDF-HFP containing only α and β phases is [30]:
(1)
F
β
=
A β
K β
K α
*
A α
+
A β
where F(β) is the content of β phase; A α and A β represent the absorbance at 763 and 840 cm−1 respectively; K α and K β are the absorption coefficients at the corresponding wavenumbers, whose values are 6.1 × 104 and 7.7 × 104 cm2 mol−1, respectively. According to the formula (1), F(β) of the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film (82.8 %) is 14.4 % larger than that of the PVDF-HFP film (72.4 %).

Compared with the PVDF-HFP film, there exist some extra peaks at 1635, 1572 and 1534 cm−1 in the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film, which probably belong to the CQDs. To verify the above speculation, FT-IR spectra of CQDs is obtained in Fig. 4(c). The broad absorption at 3450 and 3245 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. The strong peaks at 1640 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to CO and CC bands respectively, while the peaks at 1570 and 1535 cm−1 are assigned to CC and CN bonds [31]. Therefore, the extra peaks at 1635, 1572 and 1534 cm−1 in PVDF-HFP/CQDs are ascribed to the CQDs. Specially, the bands at 1635 cm−1 are assigned to COOH, while 1572 and 1534 cm−1 are attributed to CC and CN, which are consistent with the absorption peaks of CQDs in Fig. 4(c).

Lee et al. [32] have studied the annealing effect from aspects of chain orientation, crystalline morphology and polarizability of ultra-thin PVDF-TrFE film, showing that when the piezoelectric polymer is annealed above the Curie temperature and under the melting temperature for a period of time, the molecular chains in the polymer can be fully relaxed and oriented, and then the material can be better transformed into piezoelectric phase after cooling. DTA measurement was performed to investigate the annealing temperature for further improving the piezoelectric performance of these films. As seen from Fig. 4(d), the peak near 110 °C is ascribed to the Curie transition of PVDF-HFP partly overlapped with an exothermic peak [33]. Thereby, the annealing temperature of 120 °C in this study can ensure the effective thermal polarization of the films. In addition, as shown in Fig. 5 , the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm is measured to detect the specific surface area of CQDs. After calculation, the BET surface area of CQDs is 96.3 m²/g.

Hu et al. [34] reported that graphene doping can improve the crystallinity of PVDF- TrFE films by forming hydrogen bonds between graphene and PVDF-TrFE atoms, which is beneficial to improve the piezoelectric property of the films. In this study, CQDs are introduced into PVDF-HFP as a dispersion phase, and the larger specific surface area (96.3 m²/g) and abundant surface defects of CQDs will provide mass potential induced active points for the formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP. Moreover, the rich surface functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2, etc) of CQDs in the shell will promote the formation of chemical bonds between CQDs and PVDF-HFP, which effectively strengthens the interface bond. Therefore, it can be predicted that the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film will have better piezoelectric performance than the PVDF-HFP film when employed for piezoelectric sensors.

In order to investigate the piezoelectric performance of the prepared FPS, the electromechanical platform is built to measure voltage output under different simulated forces. The output voltages of PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS and PVDF-HFP based FPS by simply undergoing repeating different forces are shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). By extracting the mean values of the applied forces and the corresponding voltages, the applied force-voltage curve is obtained in Fig. 6(c). It is clear that there is a good linear relation with R2 > 0.99 between the voltage and the force for both sensors. The sensitivity of PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS (S=ΔV/ΔF, 36.6 mV/g) is 42.4 % greater than that of PVDF-HFP based FPS (25.7 mV/g). The ZJ-3AN quasi-static d33 meter was used to measure the d33 values of the piezoelectric films to confirm the piezoelectric sensor performance characteristics. The d33 value of the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film is 22.6 pC/N, which is 47.7 % larger than that of the PVDF-HFP film (15.3 pC/N). It is concluded that, due to the increased content of β phase with the addition of CQDs, which has been confirmed by XRD and FT-IR results, the piezoelectric performance of PVDF-HFP sensor could be effectively improved. In addition, the mechanical durability of the PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS is investigated by a dynamic loading test for more than 1000s under the force of 80 g (Fig. 6(d)). During the repeated loading test, the output voltages of the prepared FPS do not fluctuate significantly, indicating that the PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS have excellent electrical and mechanical stability. This is ascribed to the outstanding mechanical flexibility of PVDF-HFP and the good combination between PVDF-HFP and CQDs.

Considering the good piezoelectrical performance, mechanical durability and flexibility of the PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS, they could be applied into multiple self-powered intelligent pressure sensors. In this part, some potential applications of the prepared PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS are explored, including anti-touch alarm system, finger bending detection, voice recognition and vibration monitoring. Fig. 7 (a) presents the photograph of the FPS used for anti-touch alarm system.

The simulated touch is generated by a stepping motor. A red LED and a blue LED are reversely connected in parallel to the FPS, and the schematic diagram is shown in inset of Fig. 7(a). When the stepping motor touches the FPS, the FPS will generate a voltage signal that drives the blue LED to light first for an instant. Then when the stepper motor leaves the FPS, the red light will go on for an instant. This dynamic process is recorded in Video S1(ESI). As shown in Fig. 7(b), the design of this system can also prove the piezoelectric characteristics of PVDF-HFP material, namely the moments of applying pressure and releasing pressure will generate positive and negative voltage signals, respectively. This system can be used for anti-touch alarm in many scenarios.

The FPS is also attached on the knuckle joint to detection of finger bending (Fig. 7(c)), which could be applied in human/machine interaction. In accordance with the bending action of the finger, the FPS will generate corresponding voltage output with a very high sensitivity. As can be seen in Fig. 7(d), as the angle of knuckle bending varies from 10° to 50°, the output voltage of the sensor increases proportionally, revealing that the changes of bending angle could be quantitatively distinguished by the FPS. The related video is exhibited in Video S2(ESI). As shown in Fig. 7(e), the FPS was kept around the vocal cord to detect the real-time signals of voice. When the tester repeats 'hello', the output voltage signal detected is also periodic. The two syllables of ‘Hello’ correspond to waveform I and waveform II in Fig. 7(f). Moreover, the strength of the output signal varies somewhat with the volume of sound. In addition, the FPS was attached onto a speaker for monitoring vibration (Fig. 7(g)). In the experiment, the FPS produces an output voltage when the music is played (the drum starts), and the voltage increases with the increase of the sound vibration level. Meanwhile, the frequency of the output voltage corresponds to that of sound vibrations. The experiment data is shown in Fig. 7(h) and Video S3(ESI).

Piezoelectric PVDF-HFP/CQDs film with porous structure was fabricated through spin-coating and annealing process, which could be applied for self-powered FPS. Owing to the addition of CQDs in PVDF-HFP, the content of β phase in the PVDF-HFP/CQDs film is 14.4 % larger than that in the PVDF-HFP film. The possible reason is that the larger specific surface area (96.3 m²/g) and abundant surface defects of CQDs provide mass potential induced active points for the formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP. Moreover, the rich surface functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2, etc) of CQDs in the shell promote the formation of chemical bond between the filler and the substrate, which effectively strengthens the interface bond. Therefore, the sensitivity of PVDF-HFP/CQDs based FPS (36.6 mV/g) is 42.4 % greater than that of PVDF-HFP based FPS (25.7 mV/g) with the linearity R2 > 99 %. Besides, the FPS shows excellent mechanical stability under repeated dynamic loading. Finally, some potential applications of the PVDF-HFP/CQDs based self-powered FPS were explored, including anti-touch alarm system, finger bending detection, voice recognition and vibration monitoring.

Ping Huang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Shunjian Xu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Wei Zhong: Investigation. Haiyan Fu: Methodology, Validation. Yongping Luo: Validation. Zonghu Xiao: Resources. Meng Zhang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Education Department of Jiangxi Province (GJJ191041; KJLD13100; GJJ191052; GJJ191046; GJJ171077), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20202BABL204023) and Scientific Research Project of Xinyu University (XJZD1902; XJ1902), China.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ping Huang received his master’s degree from Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, and then worked as a lecturer in Xinyu University. He is presently studying for PhD degree in Nanchang University. His interests focus on nano materials for optoelectronic device.

Shunjian Xu received his doctor’s degree from Xi'an Jiaotong University. He is presently a professor in materials science and engineering of Xinyu University. His research interests include solar cells, flexible sensors and 3D printing.

Wei Zhong received his master’s degree from Huazhong University of science and technology. He is now a lecturer in Xinyu University. His interests focus on characterization and measurement of materials and devices.
Haiyan Fu received her master’s degree from Nanchang University. She is presently a lecturer in Xinyu University. Her interests focus on organic electronic materials and devices.
Yongping Luo received his doctor’s degree from Tongji University. He is presently a professor in chemistry of Xinyu University. His interests focus on nano materials for catalysis and sensing.
Zonghu Xiao received his doctor’s degree from Nanchang University. He is now a professor in Xinyu University. His interests focus on optoelectronic thin film and integrated devices.
Meng Zhang received her doctor’s degree from Xi'an Jiaotong University. She is presently a professor in Nanchang University. Her interests include semiconductor materials and devices.
